did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and machine learning for multiclass skin lesion recognition. Specifically, it employs fine-tuned pre-trained deep models (DarkNet-53 and DensNet-201) with residual blocks, transfer learning, feature fusion (serial-harmonic mean), feature selection using Marine Predator Optimization (MPA) and Rényi Entropy, and machine learning classifiers. The task is skin cancer/melanoma classification using ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning architecture (MSRNet) for multiclass skin lesion recognition. It details specific methods including image preprocessing, feature extraction using fine-tuned DarkNet-53 and DensNet-201, feature fusion, and feature selection using Marine Predator Optimization. The study reports original results, including accuracy metrics (85.4% on ISIC2018 and 98.80%/98.9% on ISIC2019) and comparisons with existing techniques, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: MSRNet: Multiclass Skin Lesion Recognition Using Additional Residual Block Based Fine-Tuned Deep Models Information Fusion and Best Feature Selection
brief_summary: This study proposes MSRNet, a deep learning architecture for multiclass skin lesion recognition to address the challenges of melanoma detection. The method employs fine-tuned DarkNet-53 and DenseNet-201 models with additional residual blocks, utilizing transfer learning and a novel contrast enhancement technique. Features are fused via a serial-harmonic mean approach and optimized using Marine Predator Optimization controlled by Rényi Entropy before classification with machine learning algorithms. The framework was evaluated on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets, achieving maximum accuracies of 85.4% and 98.9%, respectively. Comparative analysis demonstrates that the proposed framework outperforms several recent techniques in classification performance.

all_task_types.target: Multiclass skin lesion categories (e.g., Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) using machine learning classifiers (e.g., Cubic SVM, Quadratic SVM) on fused and selected features extracted from fine-tuned deep models (DarkNet-53, DenseNet-201). Performance is measured by accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score, and FNR.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset available for research purposes from the ISIC archive, containing images of various skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the fine-tuned DarkNet-53 and DenseNet-201 models and for experimental evaluation.
use.validation: Validation images were merged into testing images for classification results.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the proposed MSRNet framework.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset available for research purposes from the ISIC archive, containing images of various skin lesions including squamous cell carcinoma.

use.training: Used for training the fine-tuned DarkNet-53 and DenseNet-201 models and for experimental evaluation.
use.validation: Validation images were merged into testing images for classification results.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the proposed MSRNet framework.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on accuracy metrics (e.g., 85.4% vs 98.80%) without statistical significance testing.

Ablation Study (Training/Testing Ratios).description: Accuracy comparison using different training/testing ratios with feature selection technique
Ablation Study (Training/Testing Ratios).Note: Average accuracy of all classifiers was plotted for different ratios. The 50:50 approach yielded the highest average accuracy.

ISIC2019.notes: The proposed MSRNet framework achieved a maximum accuracy of 98.9% on the ISIC2019 dataset using the feature selection stage with Cubic SVM. The abstract also reports 98.80% as the maximum accuracy, likely referring to the fusion stage or a specific run, but Table 9 explicitly lists 98.9% for CSVM in the feature selection stage.

architecture_used.main_model: MSRNet (Multiclass Skin Lesion Recognition Network), a hybrid deep learning architecture combining fine-tuned DarkNet-53 and DenseNet-201 with additional residual blocks, feature fusion, and Marine Predator Optimization (MPA) for feature selection.
architecture_used.pretraining: Both DarkNet-53 and DenseNet-201 were pre-trained on the ImageNet database (more than a million images) and fine-tuned using transfer learning.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images were resized and converted to luminance channel during preprocessing).

modifications.DarkNet-53: Deleted last three layers; added three new layers; added one new residual block with three convolutional layers (filter size 3x3, stride 1); features extracted from global average pool layer (Nx1024).
modifications.DenseNet-201: Replaced last three layers with three new layers; added a residual block of six layers (including three convolutional filters 3x3, stride 1) after T3; features extracted from global average pooling layer (1920 features per image).

architecture_details.feature_fusion: Serial-harmonic mean approach to fuse features from DarkNet-53 and DenseNet-201.
architecture_details.feature_selection: Marine Predator Optimization (MPA) controlled by Rényi Entropy.
architecture_details.hyperparameter_optimization: Genetic algorithm used to select hyperparameters during the learning process.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named (standard transfer learning fine-tuning described; specific optimizer like Adam/SGD not specified for the backbone fine-tuning, though Adam was mentioned in context of other works in literature review).
training_details.data_split: 50% training, 50% testing (validation images merged into testing).
training_details.implementation_environment: MATLAB 2022b, Intel Core i7 7th gen CPU, 8 GB RAM, RTX3060 8 GB GPU.

classification_or_output_head.type: Machine Learning Classifiers (applied to extracted/fused/selected features)
classification_or_output_head.best_performing_classifier: Cubic SVM (CSVM) achieved maximum accuracy of 98.9% on ISIC2019 and 85.4% on ISIC2018 in the final feature selection stage.

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to luminance channel
Novel contrast enhancement technique based on image luminance information
Calculation of textural information using normalized luminance channels
Application of Gaussian filter for smoothing luminance image
Activation function to differentiate lesion and skin pixels

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Training and testing sets were split into 50:50 ratio before data augmentation, meaning 50% of images in each class were used for training and the remaining 50% for testing. Validation images were merged into testing images.

conclusions: The proposed MSRNet framework, which combines fine-tuned DarkNet-53 and DenseNet-201 models with additional residual blocks, serial-harmonic mean feature fusion, and Marine Predator Optimization (MPA) for feature selection, outperforms several recent techniques in multiclass skin lesion recognition.
The framework achieved a maximum accuracy of 85.4% on the ISIC2018 dataset and 98.80% (reported as 98.9% in detailed results) on the ISIC2019 dataset.

Feature fusion using the serial-harmonic mean approach improved classification accuracy by approximately 5% on ISIC2018 and 1% on ISIC2019 compared to individual deep models, but significantly increased computational time.

The feature selection stage using Marine Predator Optimization controlled by Rényi Entropy maintained consistent accuracy while significantly reducing computational time compared to the feature fusion stage.
DenseNet-201 demonstrated improved accuracy compared to DarkNet-53, but DarkNet-53 was computationally faster.

The proposed method addresses challenges in skin cancer detection such as artifacts (hairs, noise), irregular lesion shapes, and intra-class similarity by utilizing a novel contrast enhancement technique based on image luminance information.
Early and prompt diagnosis of melanoma is crucial for reducing disease severity, and the proposed deep learning architecture supports this by providing effective automated classification tools.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2018 datasets which may introduce dataset-specific bias.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the reported performance improvements over baseline methods.
The paper reports a discrepancy in the maximum accuracy on the ISIC2019 dataset, stating 98.80% in the abstract but 98.9% in the detailed results table.
The feature fusion step significantly increases computational time, which is acknowledged as a limitation of the proposed architecture.
The validation images from the ISIC datasets were merged into the testing set, which may affect the independence of the validation process.
The model was implemented using MATLAB 2022b on specific hardware (Intel Core i7, RTX3060), limiting reproducibility for users without similar computational resources.
The study addresses class imbalance issues inherent in skin lesion datasets but does not report specific metrics for minority classes beyond overall accuracy and F1-scores.
